subject: Mathematics

unit.unitTitle: Multiplying and Dividing Fractions and Decimals
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 15! In this unit you will learn how to multiply and divide unit fractions and how to multiply decimals by whole numbers. These are some of the most useful skills in all of mathematics, because they turn up every single day. When Amina measures out 3/4 of a cup of butter to bake anjero for the family, when Yusuf works out the cost of three shirts at 4.60 each at the Hargeisa market, or when Halima shares half a mango fairly between her little brothers, they are all multiplying and dividing fractions and decimals.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 5 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself on paper before you look at the solution, and only move on once you feel steady. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky, so you are never learning alone.

Why does this matter so much? Fractions and decimals are the language of measuring, sharing and money. Traders in the market use decimals to add up prices and give the right change. Cooks use fractions to scale a recipe up for a big Eid gathering or down for one person. Families share food fairly at iftar during Ramadan. Builders and tailors measure in fractions of a metre. When you become confident with these operations, you gain a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and you build the foundation you need for percentages, ratio and algebra in the years to come.

As you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you and always draw the picture. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Draw the bar, shade the parts, mark the number line, and say the answer out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure. It is simply a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
Multiply a unit fraction by a whole number, for example 1/5 × 4 = 4/5.
Divide a unit fraction by a whole number, for example 1/4 ÷ 2 = 1/8.
Multiply a decimal number (with tenths) by a whole number, for example 12.9 × 6 = 77.4.
Use bar models and number lines to represent fraction and decimal operations.
Explain the link between repeated addition and multiplication of fractions and decimals.
Estimate an answer before you calculate, and check the answer is reasonable.
Solve real-world word problems and choose the right operation each time.

concepts.title: Fractions and Decimals in Daily Life
concepts.explanation: Before we learn the methods, let us see why this maths is worth learning. Fractions and decimals are everywhere around you, hiding inside ordinary jobs you do without thinking. In the kitchen: a recipe says “use 3/4 cup of butter.” If Khadija wants to make two batches for a family gathering, she needs 3/4 × 2 = 6/4 = 1 1/2 cups. Multiplying a fraction told her exactly how much to measure.
concepts.example: At the market: one shirt costs 4.60 shillings. If Omar buys three shirts for his brothers, the total is 4.60 × 3 = 13.80. Multiplying a decimal told him the total cost so he could count out the right money.

concepts.title: Multiplying a Unit Fraction by a Whole Number
concepts.explanation: When we multiply a unit fraction by a whole number, we are finding how many of those fraction pieces we have altogether. Think of it as repeated addition, exactly the way multiplying whole numbers is repeated addition. Look at 1/5 × 4. This means “four pieces of 1/5”, which is the same as:
concepts.example: We can picture this with a bar model. Draw a whole bar and split it into 5 equal parts, because the denominator is 5. Then shade 4 of those parts, because we want four pieces of one fifth. The shaded amount is 4/5.

concepts.title: Dividing a Unit Fraction by a Whole Number
concepts.explanation: When we divide a unit fraction by a whole number, we are splitting one fraction piece into smaller equal parts. Dividing makes the pieces smaller, which is the opposite of what we did when multiplying. Here is a real story. Ibrahim has 1/3 of a chocolate bar and wants to share it equally between 2 friends. Each friend gets 1/3 ÷ 2. To see the answer, imagine the whole bar split into three thirds; now cut that one third into two equal pieces. Each little piece is 1/6 of the whole bar, so 1/3 ÷ 2 = 1/6.
concepts.example: Let us picture 1/4 ÷ 2 step by step.

concepts.title: Multiplying a Decimal by a Whole Number
concepts.explanation: Multiplying a decimal by a whole number is just like having several copies of that decimal amount. If one book costs 12.50 and Sahra buys 3 books, the total is 12.50 × 3 = 37.50. It is repeated addition again: 12.50 + 12.50 + 12.50. The key strategy: convert, multiply, convert back
concepts.example: A powerful method is to turn the decimal into a whole number, multiply, then turn it back. Let us calculate 12.9 × 6 carefully.

concepts.title: A Problem-Solving Plan
concepts.explanation: Word problems can feel harder than plain calculations, but only because you must first decide what to calculate. A clear plan removes the worry. Use these six steps every time. Read the problem carefully, more than once if needed.
concepts.example: Identify what you know and what you need to find.

concepts.title: Estimating and Checking
concepts.explanation: An estimate is a sensible guess made before you calculate. It is never a wild number; it is a thoughtful guess based on rounding to friendly numbers. Estimating protects you from big mistakes, because it tells you the size of answer to expect. To estimate a decimal multiplication, round the decimal to the nearest whole number and multiply. For 2.1 × 4, round 2.1 to 2, so the estimate is 2 × 4 = 8. When you then calculate the exact answer, 2.1 × 4 = 8.4, you can see it sits just above your estimate of 8, which is…
concepts.example: Estimating and calculating work together like two hands: one makes a quick guess, the other does the careful work, and then you compare them. If they are close, you can trust your answer. If they are far apart, stop and…

methods.title: How to multiply a decimal by a whole number
steps: Estimate by rounding the decimal to a whole number and multiplying.
Convert the decimal to a whole number (for example 1.4 = 14 ÷ 10).
Multiply the whole numbers.
Convert back by dividing by 10 (or 100 for hundredths).
Check your answer against the estimate.

methods.title: How to choose the operation in a word problem
steps: If you are collecting several equal amounts, multiply.
If you are sharing one amount into equal parts, divide.
Estimate the size of the answer before calculating.
Check the answer makes sense in the story.

methods.title: Divide a unit fraction
steps: Solution: Keep the numerator as 1 and multiply the denominator by 5: 7 × 5 = 35.
The pieces got much smaller, which makes sense because we split one seventh into five equal parts.

methods.title: A sharing story
methods.example: Tom has 1/3 of a chocolate bar and divides it equally among 4 people. What fraction of the whole bar does each person get?
steps: Solution: This is sharing, so divide: 1/3 ÷ 4.
Multiply the denominator: 3 × 4 = 12.
Each person gets 1/12 of the whole bar.
A bar model would show one third cut into four thin slices, each a twelfth of the whole.

methods.title: Multiply a decimal, convert-back method
steps: Solution: Estimate first: 2 × 6 = 12, so expect about 12.
Multiply: 18 × 6 = 108.
Convert back: 108 ÷ 10 = 10.8.

methods.title: Multiply a decimal, place value method
steps: Solution: Split 2.3 into 2 + 0.3.
Estimate check: 2 × 5 = 10, and 11.5 is just above 10 because we rounded 2.3 down, so the answer is reasonable.

workedExamples.title: Multiply a unit fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Keep the denominator (eighths) and multiply the numerator: 1 × 6 = 6. So 1/8 × 6 = 6/8, which simplifies to 3/4. Picture a bar in eighths with six parts shaded, and you can see it fills three quarters of the bar.

workedExamples.title: Multiply where the answer passes 1
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 1 × 12 = 12, so the answer is 12/9. Because 12 is bigger than 9, this is more than one whole. Rewrite it: 12/9 = 1 3/9 = 1 1/3. Multiplying a fraction can take you past one whole, and that is perfectly fine.

workedExamples.title: Divide a unit fraction
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Keep the numerator as 1 and multiply the denominator by 5: 7 × 5 = 35. So 1/7 ÷ 5 = 1/35. The pieces got much smaller, which makes sense because we split one seventh into five equal parts.

workedExamples.title: A sharing story
workedExamples.prompt: Tom has 1/3 of a chocolate bar and divides it equally among 4 people. What fraction of the whole bar does each person get?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: This is sharing, so divide: 1/3 ÷ 4. Multiply the denominator: 3 × 4 = 12. Each person gets 1/12 of the whole bar. A bar model would show one third cut into four thin slices, each a twelfth of the whole.

workedExamples.title: Multiply a decimal, convert-back method
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Estimate first: 2 × 6 = 12, so expect about 12. Convert: 1.8 = 18 ÷ 10. Multiply: 18 × 6 = 108. Convert back: 108 ÷ 10 = 10.8. Check: 10.8 is close to the estimate of 12, so it is sensible.

workedExamples.title: Multiply a decimal, place value method
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Split 2.3 into 2 + 0.3. Then (2 × 5) + (0.3 × 5) = 10 + 1.5 = 11.5. Estimate check: 2 × 5 = 10, and 11.5 is just above 10 because we rounded 2.3 down, so the answer is reasonable.

workedExamples.title: A shopping total
workedExamples.prompt: A pencil costs 1.25. How much do 4 pencils cost?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Collecting equal amounts, so multiply: 1.25 × 4. Convert: 1.25 = 125 ÷ 100. Multiply: 125 × 4 = 500. Convert back: 500 ÷ 100 = 5.00. Four pencils cost 5.00. Estimate check: about 1 × 4 = 4 to 1.5 × 4 = 6, and 5.00 sits neatly in between.

workedExamples.title: Choosing the operation
workedExamples.prompt: A recipe needs 1.5 cups of flour. For 3 batches, how much flour is needed in total?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Three equal batches means collecting, so multiply: 1.5 × 3. Split into 1 + 0.5: (1 × 3) + (0.5 × 3) = 3 + 1.5 = 4.5 cups. Estimate check: 2 × 3 = 6 as an upper guess, and 4.5 is a little below, which fits because 1.5 is below 2.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9
workedExamples.prompt: Multiply using convert-and-convert-back, showing your estimate: a) 12.9 × 6 b) 0.75 × 4.

workedExamples.title: A pencil costs 0.75. How much do 4 pencils cost
workedExamples.prompt: A pencil costs 0.75. How much do 4 pencils cost?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: Which mental strategy is best for 1/5 × 15, and what is the answer?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Calculate 1/9 × 12 and write it as a mixed number.

activities.title: Fraction Pizza Party
steps: Draw 4 circles to be pizzas.
Pizza 1: divide into 8 slices, colour 1/8 × 3 slices. Pizza 2: divide into 5 slices, colour 1/5 × 4 slices.
Pizza 3: divide into 6 slices, colour 1/6 × 2 slices. Pizza 4: divide into 4 slices, colour 1/4 × 3 slices.
Under each pizza write the multiplication and its answer.
Challenge: Which pizza has the most coloured slices? If you ate all the coloured slices, how many whole pizzas would that be altogether?

activities.title: Decimal Shopping Spree
activities.materials: paper and pencil (a calculator only for checking).
steps: Price list: pencil 0.75, eraser 1.25, notebook 2.40, ruler 1.80, glue stick 3.50.
For your group buy: 8 pencils, 2 erasers, 5 notebooks, 3 rulers, 2 glue sticks.
Multiply to find the cost of each item type (for example 0.75 × 8 for the pencils).
Add all the costs to find the total for the whole list.
Estimate first each time by rounding, then check your exact answer against the estimate.

activities.title: Fraction Division Detective
steps: Case 1: Draw a chocolate bar in 4 pieces, shade 1/4, then split that piece between 2 friends. What fraction of the whole does each friend get?
Case 2: Draw a metre line, mark 1/3, then cut that third into 3 equal pieces. What fraction of the metre is each piece?
Case 3: Draw a cookie, show 1/6, then divide it among 4 people. What fraction of the whole cookie does each person get?
Detective report: write down the pattern you notice when you divide a unit fraction by a whole number.

activities.title: Mental Math Olympics
activities.materials: a timer and paper for working.
steps: Event 1 (fractions): time yourself on 1/5 × 6, 1/8 × 3, 1/4 × 7, 1/9 × 5, 1/6 × 8. Target: under 2 minutes.
Event 2 (decimals): time yourself on 1.5 × 4, 2.3 × 3, 0.8 × 5, 1.7 × 6, 1.4 × 5. Target: under 3 minutes.
Event 3 (strategy): for 1.8 × 5, 1/4 × 12 and 2.5 × 6, write at least two ways to solve each.
Award yourself a medal: gold for beating both target times, silver for close, bronze for all answers correct.

activities.title: Pattern Discovery Investigation
steps: Fraction pattern: work out 1/2 × 1, 1/2 × 2, 1/2 × 3, 1/2 × 4, 1/2 × 5, 1/2 × 6. What happens with even and odd whole numbers?
Decimal pattern: work out 1.2 × 5, 1.4 × 5, 1.6 × 5, 1.8 × 5, 2.0 × 5. Predict 2.2 × 5 before calculating.
Division pattern: draw diagrams for 1/2 ÷ 2, 1/3 ÷ 3, 1/4 ÷ 4, 1/5 ÷ 5. Predict 1/6 ÷ 6.
Write each rule you found in your own words.

activities.title: Create Your Own Problems (Family Task)
activities.materials: paper, pencil, and a family member or friend to solve them.
steps: Write two story problems that multiply a unit fraction by a whole number, using sharing food or a craft.
Invent a mini-shop with five decimal prices, then write two problems about buying several items.
Write one problem that needs a division of a unit fraction, with a diagram to help solve it.
Give your problems to a family member to solve, then check their answers together.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you understand multiplying fractions best? Which helped with dividing fractions or multiplying decimals? What was the trickiest part, and what would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Multiplying a Unit Fraction Rule
rules.text: Keep the denominator the same. Multiply the numerator by the whole number. 1/n × a = a/n. Example: 1/5 × 4 = 4/5.

rules.title: Dividing a Unit Fraction Rule
rules.text: Keep the numerator as 1. Multiply the denominator by the whole number. 1/n ÷ a = 1/(n × a). Example: 1/4 ÷ 2 = 1/8.

rules.title: Multiplying a Decimal Rule
rules.text: Estimate first. Convert the decimal to a whole number, multiply, then convert back. Example: 12.9 × 6 = (129 × 6) ÷ 10 = 774 ÷ 10 = 77.4.

terms: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A number with a decimal point (0.5, 1.4, 12.9)
The first place after the decimal point
Adding the same number several times
A rectangle split into equal parts to show a fraction
A sensible guess made before calculating

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: An estimate is a sensible guess made before you calculate. It is never a wild number; it is a thoughtful guess based on rounding to friendly numbers. For 2.1 × 4, round 2.1 to 2, so the estimate is 2 × 4 = 8. When you then calculate the exact answer, 2.1 × 4 = 8.4, you can see it sits just above your estimate of 8, which is…

questions.question: Calculate 1/8 × 6.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 15 methods.
questions.explanation: Keep the denominator (eighths) and multiply the numerator: 1 × 6 = 6. So 1/8 × 6 = 6/8, which simplifies to 3/4. Picture a bar in eighths with six parts shaded, and you can see it fills three quarters of the bar.

questions.question: Calculate 1/9 × 12.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 15 methods.
questions.explanation: 1 × 12 = 12, so the answer is 12/9. Because 12 is bigger than 9, this is more than one whole. Rewrite it: 12/9 = 1 3/9 = 1 1/3. Multiplying a fraction can take you past one whole, and that is perfectly fine.

questions.question: Which fraction is equivalent to 1/2?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.
questions.explanation: 2/4 simplifies to 1/2, so they are equivalent.

questions.question: Write 1/10 as a decimal.
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: What is 20% of 160?
questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.hint: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

questions.question: What does “Unit fraction” mean?
options: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A number with a decimal point (0.5, 1.4, 12.9)
The first place after the decimal point
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Unit fraction means A fraction with 1 on top (1/2, 1/3, 1/5).

questions.question: What does “Numerator / Denominator” mean?
options: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A number with a decimal point (0.5, 1.4, 12.9)
The first place after the decimal point
Adding the same number several times
questions.answer: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Numerator / Denominator means Top number / bottom number of a fraction.

questions.question: What does “Decimal” mean?
options: A number with a decimal point (0.5, 1.4, 12.9)
The first place after the decimal point
Adding the same number several times
A rectangle split into equal parts to show a fraction
questions.answer: A number with a decimal point (0.5, 1.4, 12.9)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Decimal means A number with a decimal point (0.5, 1.4, 12.9).

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, then check each option.

choices: A fraction with 1 on top (1/2, 1/3, 1/5)
Top number / bottom number of a fraction
A number with a decimal point (0.5, 1.4, 12.9)
The first place after the decimal point
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Unit fraction means A fraction with 1 on top (1/2, 1/3, 1/5).

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 15 methods.
rounds.explanation: Keep the denominator (eighths) and multiply the numerator: 1 × 6 = 6. So 1/8 × 6 = 6/8, which simplifies to 3/4. Picture a bar in eighths with six parts shaded, and you can see it fills three quarters of the bar.

rounds.answer: Top number / bottom number of a fraction
rounds.clue: Use the Unit 15 Math Words & Symbols reference.
rounds.explanation: Numerator / Denominator means Top number / bottom number of a fraction.

rounds.clue: Work it out step by step, using the Unit 15 methods.
rounds.explanation: 1 × 12 = 12, so the answer is 12/9. Because 12 is bigger than 9, this is more than one whole. Rewrite it: 12/9 = 1 3/9 = 1 1/3. Multiplying a fraction can take you past one whole, and that is perfectly fine.

selfAssessment: I can this unit is designed for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, work every example yourself on paper before you look at the solution, and only move on once you feel steady. Your AI tutor is available day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a fresh way, or check your working. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky, so you are never learning alone.

I can why does this matter so much? Fractions and decimals are the language of measuring, sharing and money. Traders in the market use decimals to add up prices and give the right change. Cooks use fractions to scale a recipe up for a big Eid gathering or down for one person. Families share food fairly at iftar during Ramadan. Builders and tailors measure in fractions of a metre. When you become confident with these operations, you gain a skill you will use for the rest of your life, and you build the foundation you need for percentages, ratio and algebra in the years to come.

I can as you work, keep a pencil and paper beside you and always draw the picture. Mathematics is not a subject you can learn only by reading; you must do it. Draw the bar, shade the parts, mark the number line, and say the answer out loud. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure. It is simply a signpost showing you exactly what to practise next. Every mathematician you admire once sat where you sit now, learning these very same ideas one careful step at a time.
I can multiply a unit fraction by a whole number, for example 1/5 × 4 = 4/5.
I can divide a unit fraction by a whole number, for example 1/4 ÷ 2 = 1/8.
I can multiply a decimal number (with tenths) by a whole number, for example 12.9 × 6 = 77.4.
I can use bar models and number lines to represent fraction and decimal operations.
I can explain the link between repeated addition and multiplication of fractions and decimals.
